questions.passage:
A democracy should possess at least one public office whose holder does not govern. This claim sounds wasteful only because modern political thought has allowed administration to swallow representation. We assume that a person who speaks for the whole must also direct policy, command a party, or claim an electoral mandate. The result is predictable: every symbol of common membership becomes the property of a temporary majority. A ceremonial office can prevent that capture.

The case for such an office is not decorative. Democratic citizens disagree about laws, priorities, and leaders while remaining members of one political association. They therefore require forms through which continuity can be expressed without pretending that disagreement has vanished. A head of government cannot perform this task consistently. Even a widely respected premier is responsible for disputed choices and must defend them against opponents. Asking that same figure to embody the entire polity confuses victory with unity.

A ceremonial office separates two kinds of representation. Political representation authorizes action: budgets, appointments, statutes, negotiations. Civic representation stages membership: receiving new citizens, marking transitions, recognizing public service, and speaking at moments that concern the association rather than a party program. The first kind must be answerable to electoral conflict. The second gains value from restraint. Its dignity comes from the boundary it observes rather than from decisions it imposes. Its holder should possess enough visibility to personify continuity and too little power to convert that visibility into rule.

Skeptics dismiss ceremony as empty theater. They are half right and wholly mistaken about the consequence. Ceremony is theater, but political life cannot dispense with performance. Oaths, inaugurations, funerals, and public recognitions do not transmit information efficiently; they give durable form to relationships that otherwise remain abstract. The relevant contrast is not between theatrical politics and politics without theater. It is between performances that acknowledge their symbolic character and performances that smuggle partisan authority into the language of national identity.

Others fear that a ceremonial office will become a refuge for inherited rank or manufactured reverence. That danger is real but avoidable. The office need not be hereditary, lavish, or insulated from law. Its occupant might be selected for a fixed term by a supermajority, a rotating council, or another procedure designed to discourage factional ownership. What matters is not mystique but limitation. A ceremonial representative who can dismiss ministers, block legislation, or direct public funds is no longer merely ceremonial. Power attracts loyalty of the wrong kind.

The office must also retain the freedom to speak, though not to campaign. Silence would reduce it to furniture; constant intervention would turn it into a rival executive. Its proper voice is narrow but not trivial. It can articulate constitutional commitments, mourn losses without assigning blame, honor achievements without claiming them for a party, and remind majorities that opponents remain members of the same civic body. This language will sometimes seem vague because its subject is membership rather than policy.

Democracy does not become more honest by forcing every institution into open combat. Some distinctions are safeguards. By giving governing power to officials who can be removed and civic continuity to a figure who cannot govern, a polity admits two truths at once: majorities are entitled to act, and no majority exhausts the community. A ceremonial office is valuable precisely because it is denied the machinery that usually makes prominence dangerous.
questions.stem: Which statement best captures the author's central claim?

choices.A: Democracies benefit from separating civic representation from governing authority.
choices.B: Ceremonial offices should gradually acquire powers that elected leaders cannot exercise.
choices.C: Public rituals matter only when they communicate clear policy commitments.
choices.D: National unity requires citizens to suspend disagreement during political transitions.

explanations.A: Correct. The author argues that a visible but powerless office can represent shared civic membership without allowing a temporary majority to monopolize the whole polity.
explanations.B: The passage treats limitation as the office’s central safeguard and warns that policy powers would change its character.
explanations.C: Ceremonies are valued for giving form to civic relationships rather than for transmitting policy information.
explanations.D: The author insists that disagreement continues and seeks a form of unity that does not pretend otherwise.

questions.stem: The discussion of oaths, inaugurations, funerals, and public recognitions primarily serves to

choices.A: identify occasions when elected governments should suspend ordinary procedures.
choices.B: show that ceremonial offices require inherited traditions to remain credible.
choices.C: demonstrate that political rituals usually disguise partisan programs.
choices.D: illustrate how performance can embody civic relationships that remain abstract.

explanations.A: The passage does not advocate suspending democratic procedures on ceremonial occasions.
explanations.B: The author explicitly states that the office need not be hereditary and locates value in limitation rather than inherited rank.
explanations.C: Some performances smuggle in partisan authority, but the cited rituals are offered to show the legitimate work of ceremony.
explanations.D: Correct. These rituals do more than convey information; they make membership, transition, mourning, and recognition publicly tangible.

questions.stem: The author would most likely oppose a ceremonial representative who

choices.B: possesses authority to block legislation passed by elected officials.
choices.C: serves for a limited term selected by a broad coalition.
choices.D: recognizes public service without endorsing a party platform.

explanations.A: The author permits a narrow public voice focused on constitutional membership.
explanations.B: Correct. Blocking legislation would give the office governing power and attract partisan loyalty that undermines civic representation.
explanations.C: A fixed term chosen through a broad procedure is offered as one way to prevent factional capture.
explanations.D: Nonpartisan recognition is among the functions the passage assigns to civic representation.

questions.stem: A university wants one official to represent the institution at ceremonies without controlling budgets or academic policy. Which arrangement best follows the author's reasoning?

choices.A: The chief financial officer receives ceremonial duties because that role controls resources.
choices.B: The governing board rotates ceremonial duties among current policy advocates.
choices.C: A fixed-term representative performs civic functions while remaining outside administration.
choices.D: The university president performs all symbolic duties while defending disputed policies.

explanations.A: Control over resources combines prominence with governing power, the very fusion the author seeks to avoid.
explanations.B: Rotating among active policy advocates would make symbolic representation follow factional conflict.
explanations.C: Correct. A visible representative with a fixed term and no administrative authority parallels the proposed separation between civic continuity and governance.
explanations.D: The passage argues that an executive responsible for disputed decisions cannot consistently embody the whole association.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the author's argument?

choices.A: Citizens remember ceremonial speeches less accurately than campaign speeches.
choices.B: Nonpartisan ceremonial figures retain trust across changes in governing majorities.
choices.C: Elected leaders receive greater media attention than ceremonial representatives.
choices.D: Public rituals become shorter when attendance is voluntary.

explanations.A: Memory for speech content does not address whether ceremonial offices preserve shared civic representation.
explanations.B: Correct. Stable trust across partisan transitions would support the claim that a limited office can embody continuity without becoming the possession of one majority.
explanations.C: Different levels of attention do not establish the civic value or danger of either role.
explanations.D: Ritual length under voluntary attendance is unrelated to the proposed separation of functions.

questions.stem: In context, the statement that “power attracts loyalty of the wrong kind” means that governing authority would

choices.A: draw partisan allegiance that compromises civic neutrality.
choices.B: encourage citizens to value ceremony over policy outcomes.
choices.C: make public recognition too expensive to sustain.
choices.D: prevent the officeholder from speaking at national events.

explanations.A: Correct. Once the office can direct policy or resources, support for it will track political advantage rather than common membership.
explanations.B: The concern is not excessive love of ceremony but factional attachment to usable authority.
explanations.C: No financial consequence is attached to the phrase.
explanations.D: The author allows ceremonial speech and objects to governing power rather than public appearance.
